children.name: AI Browsers
Headless
children.short_description: Headless browsers optimised for programmatic navigation by AI agents.
children.long_description: Browser engines without a visible UI, tuned for automated page loading, DOM access, and scraping under agent control. They sit below agent frameworks as the execution substrate for web interaction, competing with general-purpose headless tools repurposed for AI use.

children.short_description: Browsers built or adapted specifically for AI agent use, including headless variants optimised for programmatic navigation.

children.name: Browser Automation
children.short_description: Libraries and services for driving real browsers under agent control.

children.name: Browser Tooling
children.short_description: Utilities and SDKs that sit alongside browsers to support agentic workflows.

children.name: Computer Use
CLI
children.short_description: CLI-based computer-use agents and tools.

children.name: Platform Specific
macOS
children.short_description: Computer-use agents built specifically for macOS desktops.
children.long_description: Agents that drive macOS via AppleScript, Accessibility APIs, or native automation hooks to control apps and the desktop. Distinct from generic vision-based computer-use by leaning on Mac-specific primitives.

children.short_description: Computer-use agents targeted at a specific operating system rather than cross-platform.
children.long_description: Groups computer-use implementations that exploit OS-native APIs, accessibility layers or UI conventions of one platform. Sits beside CLI and vision-based UI approaches as a delivery form factor for computer-use agents.

children.name: Vision UI
children.short_description: Vision-based UI understanding models for computer-use agents.
Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

children.name: Data Extraction and Structuring
children.short_description: Tools that convert unstructured data into structured form for agents to consume.

children.name: Financial Tools
children.short_description: Agent-oriented tools targeted at financial workflows.

children.name: Integrations
children.short_description: Agent-to-SaaS connector platforms: Composio-style libraries exposing third-party APIs as agent tools.

children.name: Marketplaces
children.short_description: Marketplaces and stores for discovering, listing, or transacting agents and agent-based services.

children.name: Software Extensions
children.short_description: Extensions and plugins that add agentic capabilities to existing software.

children.name: Upserting Tools
children.short_description: Tools that manage upserting data into vector stores and retrieval indexes.

children.name: Voice
children.short_description: Voice-first agents and voice interaction frameworks.

segments.name: Agent to Agent

children.name: Experiments
Voices
children.short_description: Audio-channel experiments where agents communicate via sound rather than text or structured messages.
children.long_description: Projects exploring voice or acoustic signaling as an inter-agent transport — agents emitting tones, speech, or compressed audio protocols to exchange information. A narrow experimental slot within agent-to-agent research.
children.examples_narrative: Gibberlink is the canonical example, where two voice agents detect each other and switch to a machine-efficient acoustic protocol.

children.short_description: Experimental agent-to-agent communication projects that don't fit established transport or protocol categories.
children.long_description: Research-grade and exploratory work on how agents might talk to each other — novel channels, encodings, or interaction modes. Sits alongside formal A2A protocols as a sandbox for ideas that haven't hardened into standards.

segments.short_description: Communication protocols and transports that let agents talk directly to other agents.

segments.name: Agent Tools

children.name: Comms
children.short_description: Messaging and communication tools that agents use to send, receive, or coordinate via human-style channels.
children.long_description: Services exposing email, chat, or mailbox-like primitives to agents as callable tools. Typically shipped as MCP servers or SDKs so an agent can send messages, read inboxes, and coordinate over standard comms channels.
children.examples_narrative: MCP Agent Mail illustrates the slot by giving agents a mailbox-style interface over MCP.

segments.short_description: Tools and services that agents invoke as capabilities, rather than frameworks for building agents themselves.
segments.long_description: Top-level segment for utilities designed to be called by agents — communications, side-effect actions, and similar capability providers. Distinct from runtimes, frameworks, or harnesses; these are the things at the other end of a tool call.

segments.name: Agents

children.name: Agent Clusters
children.short_description: Systems that run many agents together as a managed group or fleet.

children.name: Agent Collaboration
children.short_description: Protocols, patterns and platforms for agents to collaborate on shared tasks.

children.name: Autonomous
Trading
children.short_description: Autonomous agents that execute trading strategies in financial or crypto markets.
children.long_description: Self-directed agents that monitor markets, form views, and place trades without per-decision human approval. Typically shipped as runnable services or bots wired into exchange APIs.
children.examples_narrative: AI-Trader is an example of a goal-driven trading agent operating autonomously against market data.

children.short_description: Long-running autonomous agents that pursue goals with minimal human supervision.
children.long_description: Agents designed to operate open-endedly against a goal, planning and executing over extended horizons without turn-by-turn prompting. Distinct from chat assistants, harnesses, or task-specific agents — the defining trait is self-directed operation.

children.name: Autonomous Agent Creation
children.short_description: Agents (or platforms) whose purpose is to spawn and configure other autonomous agents.
children.long_description: Meta-level tools where the output is a new agent — skill bundles, agent factories, or systems that compose runnable agents from specs. Sits above individual agents in the stack as a creation layer.
children.examples_narrative: BankrBot Skills illustrates a skills-as-agents creation surface.

children.name: Backend Platforms
children.short_description: Self-hostable backend platforms for building, deploying, and serving LLM-powered agents and apps.
children.long_description: Full-stack platforms — usually open source, often with a UI — that provide workflow, RAG, tool, and model plumbing behind an API. Contrast with libraries (frameworks) or managed SaaS: these are deployable services developers run to host agent applications.
children.examples_narrative: Dify is a representative self-hosted agent/LLM app backend platform.

children.name: CLIs/Toolkits
Vendor
children.short_description: Vendor-published official CLI agents shipped by model providers as first-party terminal tools.
children.long_description: Official command-line agents released by the companies that train or host the underlying models. Distinguished from community CLIs by being the canonical vendor distribution.
children.examples_narrative: Qwen Code is Alibaba's official terminal coding agent for the Qwen models.

children.short_description: Command-line agents and developer toolkits for running agents from the terminal.
children.long_description: CLI-first agent surfaces — coding agents, general assistants, and SDK-style toolkits invoked from a shell. Sits at the developer-interface layer of the stack, distinct from web frontends or backend services.

children.name: Computer Use
children.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

children.name: Desktop GUIs
children.short_description: Desktop graphical interfaces for interacting with agents.

children.name: Development
children.short_description: Software-engineering agents that read, write, and modify code to complete development tasks.
children.long_description: Agents purpose-built for coding work — repo navigation, patch generation, test running, PR authoring. Distinct from general assistants by their tight integration with code, shells, and dev-loop feedback.
children.examples_narrative: OpenHands is a canonical autonomous software-engineering agent.

children.name: Domain Specific
Biotech
children.short_description: Agents specialised for biotech, bioinformatics, and life-sciences research workflows.
children.long_description: Agents built to assist with biology and biotech tasks such as literature mining, sequence analysis, lab protocol generation, and experiment planning. They typically wrap domain tools and datasets behind an LLM-driven interface.

children.short_description: Agents specialised for a particular vertical or professional domain rather than general use.
children.long_description: Parent slot for agents whose value comes from domain expertise — biotech, legal, finance, and similar. Children split by the specific field; membership implies the agent is meaningfully tailored to that domain's data, tools, or workflows.

children.name: Frameworks
Open Source
children.short_description: Open-source general-purpose agent frameworks under permissive or copyleft licences.
children.long_description: Freely available frameworks for building agents and agentic applications, distinguished from proprietary or hosted alternatives by their open codebases. They provide core loops, tool integration, and orchestration primitives developers can run and modify locally.

children.short_description: General-purpose frameworks for building agents and agentic applications.

children.name: Frontends
children.short_description: User-facing interfaces — web, desktop, mobile, terminal — for interacting with agents.

children.name: Harnesses
CLIs
Third Party
children.short_description: Third-party CLI harnesses not built by the underlying model vendor.
children.long_description: Community or independent CLI runners that host agents against one or more model providers. They offer alternative terminal UXs and feature sets distinct from vendor-shipped CLIs.

children.name: Vendor
children.short_description: Vendor-shipped CLI harnesses built by the model provider themselves.
children.long_description: Official terminal agent runners published by the company behind the underlying model, such as Google's Gemini CLI or Anthropic's Claude Code. They provide a first-party execution surface tightly coupled to the vendor's models and APIs.

children.short_description: Command-line harnesses that host and run agents in a terminal environment.
children.long_description: CLI-shaped runners that execute agents interactively or non-interactively from the shell. They sit at the execution layer of the stack, wrapping a model plus tools behind a terminal UX, and split into vendor-built and third-party implementations.

children.short_description: Test harnesses and runner frameworks that host and exercise agents.

children.name: Memory
Graph Builders
children.short_description: Memory systems that construct and maintain knowledge graphs from agent interactions.
children.long_description: Tools that extract entities and relations from conversations, documents, or events and persist them as a graph used for long-term agent memory. They sit between raw logs and retrieval, turning unstructured history into structured, queryable memory.

children.short_description: Memory systems for agents — short-term, long-term, episodic, file-backed, graph-backed.

children.name: Multiagent
Large Scale Experiments
children.short_description: Frameworks for running simulations with thousands of agents.
children.long_description: Engines built to scale multi-agent populations into the thousands or millions for social, economic, or emergent-behavior experiments. Distinct from production orchestration — the goal is research and simulation, not task delivery.

children.name: Swarm Prediction
children.short_description: Swarms of agents aggregated to produce forecasts or predictions.
children.long_description: Multi-agent systems where many independent agents vote, bid, or debate to generate a collective prediction, mimicking prediction-market or wisdom-of-crowds dynamics. The output is a forecast signal rather than a completed task.

children.short_description: Systems built around multiple cooperating or competing agents.

children.name: Networks
children.short_description: Agent networks and directories — social/professional platforms where agents and their creators are listed and interconnected.

children.name: Personification
children.short_description: Tools for giving agents distinct personas, voices and identities.

children.name: Purpose Specific
Penetration Testing
children.short_description: Agents that autonomously perform offensive security and penetration testing tasks.
children.long_description: Security-focused agents that enumerate, probe, and exploit systems in a controlled way, chaining recon and exploitation tools under LLM direction. They sit alongside traditional pentesting frameworks as an autonomous execution layer.

children.short_description: Agents built for a specific operational purpose that does not map to a domain vertical.
children.long_description: A slot for agents defined by the task they perform (e.g. penetration testing, QA, triage) rather than by industry or framework. Distinguished from Domain Specific, which groups by vertical, and from Frameworks, which group by building blocks.

children.name: Synthesised Perspectives
children.short_description: Tools that synthesise multiple viewpoints or agent outputs into a unified perspective.

children.name: Task Specific
children.short_description: Agents and tools built for a single specific task rather than general use.

children.name: Team Formation
children.short_description: Tools that assemble and configure multi-agent teams from roles or task descriptions.
children.long_description: Systems whose job is to decide which agents to spawn, with what roles, tools, and prompts, to tackle a given objective. They sit upstream of multi-agent runtimes, producing the team that another layer then executes.

children.name: Teams
children.short_description: Preconfigured multi-agent teams targeting a specific workflow or domain.
children.long_description: Ready-made collections of cooperating agents wired together for a particular use case, such as data science or content production. Distinct from Team Formation (which builds teams) and from Frameworks (which provide the primitives).

children.name: Vision
children.short_description: Agents whose primary modality is visual perception of images or video.
children.long_description: Agents built around vision models that analyse, describe, or act on image and video inputs, often as part of a broader multimodal loop. They sit alongside text and computer-use agents as a distinct perceptual slot in the stack.

segments.short_description: General agent projects and agent subtypes that do not fit more specific segments.

children.name: Automation
Open Source
GUI Based
children.short_description: Open-source visual/no-code builders for composing automation and agent workflows.
children.long_description: Drag-and-drop canvas tools where flows, triggers and agent steps are wired visually rather than in code. Typically self-hosted, targeting makers who want n8n/Zapier-style UX with open licensing.

children.short_description: Open-source automation platforms and frameworks for building agentic workflows.
children.long_description: Self-hostable automation tools that power agent-driven workflows, spanning GUI-first builders and script-based engines. Sits alongside proprietary SaaS automation platforms but distinguished by open licensing and self-hosting.

children.short_description: General-purpose automation tools and frameworks, including GUI-driven and script-driven flows.

children.name: Deployment Platforms
Serverless
children.short_description: Serverless runtimes for hosting agents without managing long-lived infrastructure.
children.long_description: Platforms that run agent workflows on-demand with managed scaling, state and scheduling. Occupies the same slot as containerised agent hosts but with a function-style execution model.

children.short_description: Platforms for deploying and hosting agents and agentic services.

children.name: Development Platforms
children.short_description: End-to-end platforms for building agentic applications.

children.name: Engineering Platforms
children.short_description: Platforms targeted at engineering teams building with LLMs and agents.

children.name: On Device Agents
Android
children.short_description: Android-specific agent runtimes and device-level automation.

children.name: SBCs
children.short_description: Agent runtimes targeting single-board computers and edge hardware.
children.long_description: Agent stacks built for Raspberry Pi class boards and similar SBCs, optimized for constrained CPU, RAM, and power budgets. Sibling to phone-specific runtimes in the on-device layer.

children.short_description: Agents designed to run on-device, including phones, SBCs and edge hardware.

children.name: Optimisation Platform
children.short_description: Platforms focused on optimising agent/LLM pipelines.

children.name: Platforms
children.short_description: General platforms that host or enable agentic applications.

children.name: SaaS Builders
Code-Defined
children.short_description: Hosted builders where agents and workflows are primarily defined in code (SDK, DSL, or config-as-code).

children.name: No Code
children.short_description: Builders targeted at non-programmers, typically form- and config-driven with minimal visual wiring.

children.name: Visual Programming
Workflow Focused
children.short_description: Visual builders oriented around structured, multi-step workflows rather than free-form agents.
Builders where flows are expressed as node-and-edge graphs or canvas-based visual programs.
Hosted SaaS platforms for building agents and agentic workflows, spanning no-code, visual, and code-defined paradigms.

children.name: Context
Context Optimisation
children.short_description: Tools that shrink, prioritise or restructure context before it reaches the model.
children.long_description: Middleware focused on making the context window more efficient — pruning, compressing, reordering or mode-switching content for an LLM call. Sits between context assembly and the model itself.

children.name: Engines
children.short_description: Runtime engines that assemble and serve context to agents on demand.
children.long_description: Backends that fetch, merge and format context from multiple sources into a prompt-ready payload. The server-side counterpart to context optimisation and visualisation tools.

children.name: Frontend Tools
children.short_description: Client-side tools for capturing or selecting context from a running UI.
children.long_description: Browser/app-side utilities that let users grab DOM fragments, components or page state and feed them into an agent. Typically a bridge between a live frontend and a context engine.

children.name: Tools
Context Managers
children.short_description: Utilities that organise, persist and version the context a developer feeds into coding agents.
children.long_description: Tooling for curating dotfile-style context bundles, rules and memory snippets used by AI coding assistants. Acts as a local source of truth for what gets injected into each session.

children.short_description: Utility tools, SDKs and toolkits for building, debugging and extending agentic systems.

children.name: Visualisation
children.short_description: Tools for inspecting and visualising what is inside an LLM context window.
children.long_description: Debug and introspection utilities that render tokens, messages and attached artefacts so developers can see exactly what the model received. Complements context optimisation by exposing waste and overlap.

children.short_description: Tools for building, optimising, managing and visualising the context window supplied to LLMs.

children.name: Embedding Pipeline Orchestrators
children.short_description: Orchestration tools that manage embedding generation and ingestion pipelines.

children.name: Memory
File
children.short_description: File-backed memory stores for agents.
children.long_description: Memory backends that persist agent state to files — flat, encoded, or media-container formats — rather than databases or vector stores. Useful for portable, inspectable, or offline agent memory.

children.name: Regressive
children.short_description: Memory that refines itself by regressing over past interactions.
children.long_description: Memory systems that iteratively re-process prior context to extract, correct, or compress learned signal, rather than just appending new entries. Targets long-running agents whose context quality must improve over time.

children.name: Skills
children.short_description: Agent skills ecosystems — registries, bundles, standards, guides and package libraries.
Memory systems for agents — short-term, long-term, episodic, file-backed, graph-backed.

children.name: Personal AI
children.short_description: Personal AI assistants and related projects.

children.name: RAG
Ingestion
children.short_description: Pipelines that ingest and process documents into retrieval systems.
Retrieval-augmented generation engines, frameworks and components.

children.name: Semantic Search
children.short_description: Semantic search tools for agents operating over codebases and local content.

children.name: Vector Databases
children.short_description: Vector databases used for embedding storage and similarity search.

children.name: Dev Tools
Debugging
children.short_description: Debugging and introspection tools for agent development.

children.name: IDE Integrations
children.short_description: IDE extensions and plugins for agent-assisted development.

children.name: Repo Refactoring
children.short_description: Agent-assisted tooling for restructuring and refactoring entire repositories.
children.long_description: Tools that apply large-scale, agent-driven transformations across a codebase — renames, moves, config migrations, architectural shifts. Distinct from IDE-level assistants by operating at repo scope.

children.short_description: Developer tooling that uses agents or supports agent development workflows.

children.name: Skills
Standards
children.short_description: Proposed and emerging standards across the agentic ecosystem.
Agent skills ecosystems — registries, bundles, standards, guides and package libraries.

children.name: Environments
OS
children.short_description: Agent-native operating systems providing a full OS abstraction for autonomous agents to run within.
children.long_description: OS-level environments purpose-built as runtimes for agents, exposing processes, filesystem, and system calls as primitives the agent can control. Sits below frameworks and harnesses as the host substrate.

children.name: Remote Desktops
children.short_description: Hosted remote desktop / VM services providing agents with a persistent GUI environment to operate.
Sandboxed environments — OS, simulated worlds, virtual machines — in which agents run.

children.name: Frameworks
.NET
children.short_description: .NET/C# agent frameworks for building agents in the Microsoft ecosystem.
children.long_description: Agent-building frameworks targeting the .NET runtime and C# developers. Provides idiomatic APIs, DI, and tooling for embedding LLM agents into .NET applications and services.

children.name: Computer Use
children.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

children.name: Multiagent Orchestration
children.short_description: Frameworks for coordinating multiple cooperating agents with roles, messaging, and task delegation.
children.long_description: Frameworks whose primary abstraction is a team of agents: role assignment, inter-agent messaging, shared state, and task routing. Used to compose workflows where specialised agents collaborate rather than a single monolithic agent.

children.name: Multimodal
children.short_description: Agent frameworks with first-class support for non-text modalities such as audio, image, and video.
children.long_description: Frameworks designed around multimodal input and output — video, image, audio streams — rather than text-only chat. Handles media I/O plumbing, streaming, and model routing for agents that perceive or produce rich media.

children.name: Optimisation
Parallelisation
children.short_description: Frameworks that parallelise agent or subtask execution across workers for throughput.
children.long_description: Tools that fan out agent work — steps, tool calls, or whole agent runs — across parallel workers. Targets throughput-bound workloads where serial agent loops are the bottleneck.

children.short_description: Frameworks focused on optimising agent execution — latency, cost, throughput, parallelism.
children.long_description: Frameworks whose differentiator is execution efficiency rather than abstractions for authoring agents. Covers parallel dispatch, batching, and cost/latency tradeoffs.

children.name: Social Simulations
children.short_description: Frameworks for simulating populations of agents modelling social, economic, or behavioural dynamics.
children.long_description: Frameworks for agent-based social simulation: many LLM-backed personas interacting under rules to study emergent group behaviour. Used for research, game-like worlds, and counterfactual studies rather than task execution.

children.name: Stateful Design
children.short_description: Frameworks built around persistent, long-lived agent state and memory as the core abstraction.
children.long_description: Frameworks where durable agent state — memory, identity, long-running context — is the central primitive rather than a bolt-on. Agents persist across sessions and evolve their internal state over time.

children.name: Voice
children.short_description: Voice-first agents and voice interaction frameworks.
General-purpose frameworks for building agents and agentic applications.

children.name: Harnesses
Checkpointing
children.short_description: Save and restore agent execution state across interruptions, crashes, or session boundaries.

children.name: Context Compression
children.short_description: Harness-level summarisation and pruning that rolls the context window during a run, distinct from gateway-level compression in the request path.

children.name: Deep Research
children.short_description: Harnesses for multi-step research workflows with planning, tool use, and synthesis.

children.name: Platform Specific
macOS
children.short_description: Harnesses that run agents natively on macOS.
Harnesses that target a specific host OS or environment for native integration.

children.name: Retry and Recovery
children.short_description: Failure handling, automatic rerun on tool errors, and partial-result recovery for agent loops.

children.name: Sub-agent Dispatch
children.short_description: Libraries and runtimes that spawn isolated sub-agent contexts to offload work and preserve main-thread coherence.

children.name: Task Coherence
children.short_description: Progress trackers, self-reflection loops, and plan-revision utilities that keep long-running agent tasks on course.
Execution-layer scaffolding that wraps agents for real runs — sub-agent dispatch, long-horizon coherence, checkpointing, platform-specific runtimes.
children.long_description: Harnesses sit between frameworks (which define agent shape) and runtimes (which provide the sandbox). They're the execution-layer utilities that make long-running, multi-step agent runs actually work in practice: spawning isolated sub-agent contexts, resuming from checkpoints, recovering from tool failures, keeping the main thread coherent over time, and adapting to the host platform.

children.name: Loops
children.short_description: Agent loop implementations — the core think/act/observe cycle.

children.name: Orchestration
Platform-Specific
children.short_description: Orchestration frameworks tied to a specific cloud or vendor platform.
children.long_description: Agent orchestration engines that are coupled to one provider's runtime, SDKs, or services rather than being platform-neutral. Chosen when a team has already committed to that platform's ecosystem.

children.short_description: Orchestration engines for coordinating agents, tools and workflows.

children.name: Runtimes
Policy Engines
children.short_description: Runtime policy engines that evaluate and enforce rules on agent actions.
Execution runtimes — sandboxes, data layers, kernels — that host running agents.

children.name: Starter Kits
children.short_description: Opinionated starter kits and templates for kicking off agentic projects.

segments.name: Frontends

children.name: Android
children.short_description: Native Android apps that serve as frontends for interacting with agents on mobile.
children.long_description: Android-specific client applications for chatting with and operating agents from a phone. Handles mobile UX, background behaviour, and on-device integrations distinct from web or desktop clients.

children.name: Chat UIs
children.short_description: Web or desktop chat interfaces purpose-built as frontends for agents rather than plain LLMs.
children.long_description: Chat-style user interfaces tailored for agent interaction — tool call rendering, step traces, artefact display — rather than generic LLM chat. Typically web-based and framework-agnostic on the backend.

children.name: GUIs and CLIs
children.short_description: Dual GUI-and-CLI agent clients offering both graphical and command-line interaction modes.
children.long_description: Agent frontends that ship as both a graphical desktop app and a command-line tool, sharing a backend. Lets users pick interaction style per task without switching agents.

children.name: TUIs and CLIs
children.short_description: Terminal user interfaces and command-line clients for interacting with agents.
children.long_description: Text-mode frontends — TUIs and CLIs — for driving agents from the terminal. Favoured by developers for scriptability, low overhead, and integration into shell workflows.

segments.short_description: User-facing interfaces — web, desktop, mobile, terminal — for interacting with agents.

children.name: Data Sources
Financial
children.short_description: Financial and markets data feeds exposed as tools or sources for agents.
children.long_description: Providers of equities, crypto, macro and company fundamentals data packaged for programmatic agent consumption. Sits in the data layer beneath research and trading agents.

children.short_description: Curated data feeds and providers that agents can draw on.

children.name: Gateways
Context Compression
children.short_description: Gateways that compress or distill context before it reaches the model.
children.long_description: Proxy-layer services that sit between agents and LLMs to shrink prompts, summarize history, or prune tool output, reducing token spend and latency. Distinct from memory systems in that they operate inline on the request path rather than storing state.

children.short_description: Gateway services that sit between agents and upstream services (LLMs, tools, data).

children.name: Inference Servers
children.short_description: LLM inference servers and runtimes (vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp, SGLang) that agents call into.

children.name: Proxy
Gateways
children.short_description: Gateway services that sit between agents and upstream services (LLMs, tools, data).
Proxy services that sit between clients and LLM/agent backends.

children.name: Routers
children.short_description: Routing layers that select between models, tools or agents based on cost, capability or intent.

children.name: Semantic Caching
children.short_description: Cache layers that serve semantically equivalent LLM responses to cut cost and latency.

children.name: Annotation
children.short_description: Human labeling, RLHF, and feedback-collection platforms for agent training data.

children.name: UX
Human in the Loop
children.short_description: Interfaces and workflows for inserting humans into agent execution — approvals, task handoff, review queues.
User-experience patterns and components specifically for agent systems.

children.name: Workspaces
children.short_description: Shared workspaces where agents and humans (or multiple agents) collaborate.

segments.name: MCP

children.name: Aggregation
children.short_description: Aggregators that bundle many MCP servers behind a single endpoint.
children.long_description: Meta-MCP services that expose dozens or hundreds of upstream MCP servers through one unified connection, handling discovery, auth, and routing. Reduces per-client configuration burden when an agent needs access to a broad tool surface.

children.name: Gateways
children.short_description: Gateway services that sit between agents and upstream services (LLMs, tools, data).

children.name: Third Party
children.short_description: Community-built MCP servers wrapping third-party SaaS and APIs.
children.long_description: Unofficial MCP server implementations that expose external products and APIs (Airbnb, Spotify, etc.) as MCP tools, built outside the vendor. The catch-all slot for community MCP coverage of commercial services.

segments.short_description: Projects in the Model Context Protocol ecosystem — servers, gateways, aggregators, clients.

children.name: Embedding Models
children.short_description: Models used to produce embeddings for retrieval and RAG pipelines.

children.name: LLMs
Agent Specific
children.short_description: LLMs trained or tuned specifically for agentic and multi-agent workloads.
children.long_description: Models whose training objective, post-training, or native harness targets tool use, planning, or multi-agent coordination rather than general chat. Sits at the model layer of the stack and is chosen when vanilla instruction-tuned models fall short on agent benchmarks.

children.name: By Use Case
Computer Use
children.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).
LLMs grouped by the agentic use case they are optimized for.
children.long_description: Organizes models by the downstream agentic task they target — computer use, coding, browsing, and so on — rather than by vendor or size. A parent bucket whose children are the specific use-case slots.

children.short_description: Language models, including those trained or tuned specifically for agentic use cases.

children.name: Reinforcement Learning
children.short_description: RL frameworks and environments used to train agentic behaviour.

children.name: Synthetic Data
children.short_description: Tools for generating synthetic training and evaluation data for agents.

children.name: Trainers
children.short_description: Training frameworks and tools for agent models.

segments.name: Observability

children.name: Agent Analytics
children.short_description: Product analytics for agent behaviour: funnels, retention, conversation quality, task success.

children.name: Benchmarks
children.short_description: Standardised benchmarks for evaluating agent and model capabilities.

children.name: Evals and Experiment Tracking
Frameworks
children.short_description: General-purpose frameworks for building agents and agentic applications.

children.name: Observability
children.short_description: Logging, tracing and observability specifically for agent and LLM systems.
Frameworks, harnesses and platforms for evaluating agents and tracking experiments.

children.name: Operations
Cost Monitoring
children.short_description: Tools that track token spend and cost for production agents.
children.long_description: Observability systems focused on financial metrics — per-run, per-agent, and per-tool cost attribution for LLM and tool calls. Distinct from general tracing or evals; the primary surface is a spend dashboard.

children.short_description: Operational concerns for running agents in production — cost, monitoring, reliability.

segments.short_description: Logging, tracing and observability specifically for agent and LLM systems.

children.name: Foundations
children.short_description: Industry foundations, consortia and non-profits shaping the agentic AI ecosystem.

segments.name: Pipelines
segments.short_description: Reusable pipelines for common agentic workloads.

children.name: Conversational Control
children.short_description: Systems that steer or constrain multi-turn conversations with agents.

children.name: Feedback Formatting
children.short_description: Tools that shape feedback signals (tool output, user input) into forms agents can act on.

children.name: Formatting
children.short_description: Token-efficient serialisation and data-formatting schemes for passing structured data to and from LLMs.

children.name: Prompt Management
children.short_description: Versioning, registries, and lifecycle management for prompts as first-class artifacts.

children.name: Structured Output
children.short_description: Libraries that constrain LLM outputs to typed schemas, JSON, or formal grammars.

children.name: Workflow Definition
children.short_description: Tools and DSLs for authoring agent workflows as first-class, portable artifacts.

children.name: Guardrails
children.short_description: Guardrails and pre-execution safety checks that gate what running agents can do.

children.name: Identity and Auth
children.short_description: Agent identity, credential vaulting, and delegated authentication for tool access.

children.name: PII and Redaction
children.short_description: PII detection, content sanitization, and safety filtering for agent I/O.

children.name: Security
Automation
children.short_description: General-purpose automation tools and frameworks, including GUI-driven and script-driven flows.

children.name: Control Planes
children.short_description: Centralised control planes for governing, auditing, and enforcing policy across agent fleets.
Security tooling for agents: sandboxing, red teaming, defensive automation.

children.name: Geopolitical Simulation
children.short_description: Simulations focused specifically on geopolitical scenarios and dynamics.

children.name: Societal Simulations
children.short_description: Large-scale simulations of societies and human behaviour using agent populations.

children.name: World Generation
MCP
children.short_description: MCP servers for world and scene generation.
Tools and models for generating simulated worlds for agents to operate in.

segments.name: Standards

children.name: Emerging (Ecosystem-Tied)
children.short_description: Emerging standards whose adoption is tied to a specific host ecosystem (e.g. a browser engine or platform vendor) rather than being fully vendor-neutral.

children.name: Ratified
children.short_description: Standards with a published specification and active governance.

segments.short_description: Proposed and emerging standards across the agentic ecosystem.

children.name: Backend Querying
children.short_description: Tools that let agents query backend systems, databases and APIs.

children.name: Databases
Context Databases
children.short_description: Databases specifically designed for storing and retrieving agent/LLM context.
Database systems used in agentic stacks, including purpose-built context and vector stores.

segments.name: Uncategorized
segments.short_description: Holding area for projects awaiting classification. Entries here are a todo list, not a destination.
